content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
107 SOUTH BROADWAY • • • SUITE 7007 • • • LOS ANGELES, 90012

July 15, 1980


Ms. Emily Demir
Government Relations Department
SHAKLEE CORPORATION
1900 Powell Street
Emeryville, California 94608

Dear Ms. Demir:

Pursuant to your request, this letter is to verify
Nan Emmerson's previous advice as well as my advice to
you during our conversation of July 14, 1980.

The question you posed previously to Nan Emmerson,
and requested verification of from myself was, whether or
not the Political Reform Act prohibited a state registered
political action committee from making contributions to
out-of-state candidates or committees?

I advised that the Political Reform Act does not
contain any prohibition of a state registered political
action committee from making any out-of-state contributions.
However, since the committee is registered as a recipient
or political action committee, under Government Code Section
82013(a), the committee is obligated to report those
expenditures pursuant to Government Code Section 84210
on the next campaign statement required by the Political
Reform Act. You might also check with the state to which
you are to make the contribution for any possible filing
obligation there.

I hope this information has been helpful. Please
feel free to contact me if I can be of any further
assistance.

Sincerely,

Helen P. Arriola
Political Reform
Consultant

HPA:jg

SACRAMENTO (916) 322-5660 • LOS ANGELES (213) 620-5160

sections.question: Whether or not the Political Reform Act prohibited a state registered political action committee from making contributions to out-of-state candidates or committees?
sections.conclusion: The Political Reform Act does not contain any prohibition of a state registered political action committee from making any out-of-state contributions. However, since the committee is registered as a recipient, or political action committee, under Government Code Section 82013(a), the committee is obligated to report those expenditures pursuant to Government Code Section 84210 on the next campaign statement required by the Political Reform Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Does the California Political Reform Act prohibit a state-registered political action committee from making contributions to out-of-state candidates or committees?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Political Reform Act does not prohibit out-of-state contributions by registered political action committees, but such expenditures must be reported on campaign statements as required by Government Code Section 84210. The recipient should also verify compliance with filing obligations in the recipient state.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document has significant OCR errors and scanning artifacts (missing/garbled text). Question and conclusion sections were reconstructed from context. Dated July 15, 1980, addressed to Shaklee Corporation. Signed by Helen P. (Political Consultant). Letter verifies previous advice from Nan Emmerson.

QUESTION: Whether or not the Political Reform Act prohibited a state registered political action committee from making contributions to out-of-state candidates or committees?

CONCLUSION: The Political Reform Act does not contain any prohibition of a state registered political action committee from making any out-of-state contributions. However, since the committee is registered as a recipient, or political action committee, under Government Code Section 82013(a), the committee is obligated to report those expenditures pursuant to Government Code Section 84210 on the next campaign statement required by the Political Reform Act.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises that the Political Reform Act does not prohibit state-registered political action committees from making contributions to out-of-state candidates or committees, but such expenditures must be reported on campaign statements. The committee should also verify compliance requirements in the recipient state.
